EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 4 PM CDT on Wednesday afternoon the 25th and 130 AM CDT on early Thursday morning the 26th, the majority of this six-county area experienced two separate rounds of fairly widespread strong to severe multicell storms. The first round peaked in intensity between 430 PM and 8 PM CDT, developing in response to intense surface heating as temperatures soared into the 100-110F range near a slow-moving cold front. Although radar signatures suggest that severe-criteria gusts of 60 MPH or higher might have affected parts of several counties during this late afternoon/early evening time frame, the only ground-truth reports came out of Mitchell County including a caved-in garage door south of Beloit and a round hay bale rolled across a road near Simpson. Once this initial round of convection moved out of the area to the south and east, a lull ensued before the second round of strong to severe convection flared up after nightfall. This round peaked in intensity between 1030 PM and 1 AM CDT and again consisted of a number of multicell storm clusters and short line segments. Although reports of severe weather were again limited, these nighttime storms dropped golf ball size hail in the community of Jewell and produced a 69 MPH measured gust at the Rooks County airport south of Stockton. Somewhere along the way, a few power poles were also snapped off several miles west of Stockton. Event-total rainfall amounts were highly variable across the area, but most places averaged less than 1 inch. However, considerably higher totals were measured in southern Jewell County, including 3.87 at Jewell and 2.83 in Ionia. ||In the mid and upper levels the flow was quasi-zonal, with the primary synoptic forcing for this event consisting of subtle shortwave energy passing through the Central Plains along the southern periphery of a dominant upper low centered near the North Dakota-Manitoba border. At the surface, a slow moving cold front gradually passed through North Central Kansas during the afternoon and evening. Although deep-layer vertical wind shear was rather meager at only around 25 knots at best, at least 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE in the presence of a hot and deeply-mixed airmass with surface temperature-dewpoint depressions on the order of 40-50F was quite favorable for downbursts, especially with the initial round of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts measured to at least 60 mph by a home weather station resulted in tree damage and a caved-in garage door.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level high pressure ridge over the central portions of the country caused more dry conditions for the area. Severe to exceptional drought remained in place across extreme southeastern Kansas. Some rainfall fell by the end of the month for portions of the area. For the counties of Bourbon, Crawford and Cherokee in southeastern Kansas, between 50 and 75 percent of the combined corn, soybeans and hay acreage was reported as a loss due to the drought.  The losses and damages to harvested yield were from the start of the planting season through the end of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor continued to report Extreme Drought (D3) to Exceptional Drought (D4) throughout the month of August. The region started to see some rainfall by the end of August. The COOP station near Columbus reported 3.19 of rainfall for the month of August. This is a continuation of the drought that began across the region in June. The USDA office in Cherokee county estimated a loss of 60% of the corn crop and and 50% of the soybean crop.  Hay and pasture grass loss was at 50%. Monetary crop loss figures are estimates using information from the National Agricultural Statistics database, local FSA and USDA offices and other local, state or federal agency information. Livestock losses, if they occurred or were reported are listed in the property section.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This entire six-county portion of North Central Kansas started out July in category D2 severe drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, but by month's end most of the area had degraded to category D3 extreme drought for the first time since at least early-2004, with the exception of Jewell County which remained at D2. As of July 31st, all six counties were either a primary or contiguous county in a federally-declared drought disaster area. This onset of severe drought represented a notable degradation in conditions since early May when the entire area was free of all drought categories. Looking back a few months prior to the onset of drought, a fairly wet April helped mitigate the impact of a rather dry March. As a result, the period from May into early June would prove critical as to whether drought would develop. Unfortunately, the combined months of May and June subsequently yielded no more than 15-35% of normal precipitation across most of the area. The situation continued to worsen in July, with most of the area measuring no more than 1-2 inches of rain, versus the 30-year normal of around 4 inches. A few of the lowest cooperative observer rainfall totals during July included 0.27 at Hunter, 0.91 at Burr Oak and 0.92 at Covert. Despite the overall dryness, there were localized exceptions of heavier monthly rainfall, including Jewell which stood out with 5.16. However, the lack of rainfall was not the only factor contributing to the worsening drought. Average daily maximum temperatures for the combined months of June and July ended up a significant 6-10 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. Near Alton, the average June-July daily high temperature was 98.7 degrees, or 9.6 degrees hotter than normal. Between July 13th-26th, Alton reached at least 100 degrees 14 days in row, the longest such streak since 1980. For the week ending July 29th, the Kansas Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that topsoil moisture across the north central district was 85 percent short to very short and subsoil moisture 89 percent short to very short. Across the state as a whole, range and pasture conditions were reported to be 88 percent poor to very poor, the worst since ratings began in 1995. The Kansas corn and soybean crop was rated as 66 percent and 59 percent poor to very poor, respectively. The Kansas report also indicated that many livestock producers were selling calves earlier than preferred and were culling herds as pasture conditions worsened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Smith County started out the month designated in the severe drought category (D2), but ended the month designated as extreme (D3).

The Kansas corn and soybean crop was rated as 66 percent and 59 percent poor to very poor, respectively. The Kansas report also indicated that many livestock producers were selling calves earlier than preferred and were culling herds as pasture conditions worsened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rooks County started out the month designated in the severe drought category (D2), but ended the month designated as extreme (D3).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This entire six-county portion of North Central Kansas started out July in category D2 severe drought according to the U.S. Drought Monitor, but by month's end most of the area had degraded to category D3 extreme drought for the first time since at least early-2004, with the exception of Jewell County which remained at D2. As of July 31st, all six counties were either a primary or contiguous county in a federally-declared drought disaster area. This onset of severe drought represented a notable degradation in conditions since early May when the entire area was free of all drought categories. Looking back a few months prior to the onset of drought, a fairly wet April helped mitigate the impact of a rather dry March. As a result, the period from May into early June would prove critical as to whether drought would develop. Unfortunately, the combined months of May and June subsequently yielded no more than 15-35% of normal precipitation across most of the area. The situation continued to worsen in July, with most of the area measuring no more than 1-2 inches of rain, versus the 30-year normal of around 4 inches. A few of the lowest cooperative observer rainfall totals during July included 0.27 at Hunter, 0.91 at Burr Oak and 0.92 at Covert. Despite the overall dryness, there were localized exceptions of heavier monthly rainfall, including Jewell which stood out with 5.16. However, the lack of rainfall was not the only factor contributing to the worsening drought. Average daily maximum temperatures for the combined months of June and July ended up a significant 6-10 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. Near Alton, the average June-July daily high temperature was 98.7 degrees, or 9.6 degrees hotter than normal. Between July 13th-26th, Alton reached at least 100 degrees 14 days in row, the longest such streak since 1980. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 4 PM CDT on Wednesday afternoon the 25th and 130 AM CDT on early Thursday morning the 26th, the majority of this six-county area experienced two separate rounds of fairly widespread strong to severe multicell storms. The first round peaked in intensity between 430 PM and 8 PM CDT, developing in response to intense surface heating as temperatures soared into the 100-110F range near a slow-moving cold front. Although radar signatures suggest that severe-criteria gusts of 60 MPH or higher might have affected parts of several counties during this late afternoon/early evening time frame, the only ground-truth reports came out of Mitchell County including a caved-in garage door south of Beloit and a round hay bale rolled across a road near Simpson. Once this initial round of convection moved out of the area to the south and east, a lull ensued before the second round of strong to severe convection flared up after nightfall. This round peaked in intensity between 1030 PM and 1 AM CDT and again consisted of a number of multicell storm clusters and short line segments. Although reports of severe weather were again limited, these nighttime storms dropped golf ball size hail in the community of Jewell and produced a 69 MPH measured gust at the Rooks County airport south of Stockton. Somewhere along the way, a few power poles were also snapped off several miles west of Stockton. Event-total rainfall amounts were highly variable across the area, but most places averaged less than 1 inch. However, considerably higher totals were measured in southern Jewell County, including 3.87 at Jewell and 2.83 in Ionia. ||In the mid and upper levels the flow was quasi-zonal, with the primary synoptic forcing for this event consisting of subtle shortwave energy passing through the Central Plains along the southern periphery of a dominant upper low centered near the North Dakota-Manitoba border. At the surface, a slow moving cold front gradually passed through North Central Kansas during the afternoon and evening. Although deep-layer vertical wind shear was rather meager at only around 25 knots at best, at least 1000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE in the presence of a hot and deeply-mixed airmass with surface temperature-dewpoint depressions on the order of 40-50F was quite favorable for downbursts, especially with the initial round of storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 mph.  A round hay bale rolled across the road near Greenwood Cemetary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent upper level high pressure ridge over the central portions of the country caused more extreme heat and dry conditions for the area. Severe drought remained in place across extreme southeastern Kansas as rain fell sparsely during the month. Many areas saw one of the driest Julys on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor continued to report Severe Drought (D2) throughout the month of July. The region saw very little in the way of rainfall making it one of the driest July's on record.  The COOP station near Columbus reported 1.33 of rainfall for the month of July. This is a continuation of the drought that began across the region in June. The loss and damage estimates will be included in the August storm data section for the entire drought event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure settled over the central portions of the U.S. and became the dominant weather pattern for much of the Summer of 2012. Several record highs were broken around the area and high temperatures were above 100 degrees 17 days of the month. The overall mean temperatures in nearby Joplin was 6.4 degrees above normal for the month of July. There were 31 days where the maximum temperatures reached 90 degrees or above.  The hottest temperature record during the month was 109 degrees on July 29th. Heat advisories and warnings were issued for portions of the area by the end of June. Even though air temperatures were extreme, the humidity levels were relatively dry causing the heat index to be closer to the actual air temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions, which started in the spring, intensified during the month of July. Drought conditions expanded across Kansas, with D2 conditions at the beginning of the month, increasing to D3 conditions by the end of the month. Most locations by the end month, had yearly rainfall deficits of 10 to 15 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions became worse during the month as D3 conditions developed. Atchison reported 1.61 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Stratiform rain caused strong winds to reach the surface in two known locations, one being near Junction City, Kansas, and the other being near Rantoul, Kansas. These weakly convecting showers caused strong winds which caused damage in these two locations, highlighted by power poles and lines down near Junction City and a barn being blown down near Rantoul.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weakly convecting showers caused strong winds which blew down a barn, along with some tree limbs of unknown diameter.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: As a result of weakly convecting stratiform rain showers 3 power poles were reported down at Fort Riley near the PX.  Other tree branches of unknown diameters were broken as a result of the strong winds associated with the weak showers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A more active weather pattern occurred during the month of October with several rounds of appreciable rainfall. Drought conditions continued to slowly improve over portions of extreme southeastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor continued to report Severe Drought (D2) to Extreme Drought (D3) throughout the month of October. The COOP station near Fort Scott reported 3.35 of rainfall for the month of October. This is a continuation of the drought that began across the region in June. The loss and damage estimates will be included in August's storm data section for the entire drought event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although thunderstorms were rather numerous across the six-county area for several hours on this Thursday afternoon and evening, the vast majority of this activity remained sub-severe. However, one storm briefly became severe over Rooks County shortly before 6 PM CDT, resulting in a report of estimated 60 MPH winds and nickel size hail near Stockton. For most of the area this round of storms ended up being nothing more than a very beneficial rain event, with widespread coverage of at least 0.75 to 1.50 inches. There were even localized pockets of much heavier rainfall, including 3.43 inches reported by a CoCoRaHS observer 10 miles west-northwest of Stockton. Scattered to numerous strong multicell storms first started developing across North Central Kansas between 430 PM and 530 PM CDT, eventually coalescing into a larger mass of showers and weak storms that lingered over parts of the area until around midnight CDT. In the mid and upper levels, flow was quasi-zonal and fairly weak, but did contain embedded subtle shortwave troughs. At the surface, the initial storms flared up in the vicinity of a rather well-defined trough axis extending northeastward from southwest Kansas, and also on the warm side of a differential heating boundary separating mostly sunny skies across most of Kansas from widespread cloud cover over southern Nebraska. Around the time of the severe storm in Rooks County, the mesoscale environment featured at least 1000-1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, but no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell along with 60 mph wind gusts.

At the surface, the initial storms flared up in the vicinity of a rather well-defined trough axis extending northeastward from southwest Kansas, and also on the warm side of a differential heating boundary separating mostly sunny skies across most of Kansas from widespread cloud cover over southern Nebraska. Around the time of the severe storm in Rooks County, the mesoscale environment featured at least 1000-1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE, but no more than 30 knots of 0-6 kilometer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total rainfall of 3.43 inches was reported during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A more active weather pattern occurred during the month of October with several rounds of appreciable rainfall. Drought conditions continued to slowly improve over portions of extreme southeastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor continued to report Severe Drought (D2) to Extreme Drought (D3) throughout the month of October. The COOP station near Columbus reported 2.63 of rainfall for the month of October. This is a continuation of the drought that began across the region in June. The loss and damage estimates will be included in August's storm data section for the entire drought event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly between midnight and 2 AM CDT on this early Thursday morning, the southern edge of a storm complex primarily centered over southern Nebraska brushed through portions of this six-county area, primarily affecting Phillips, Smith, Jewell and Mitchell Counties. Although ground-truth reports were quite limited, the NWS cooperative observer at Burr Oak reported several tree branches down from estimated 60 MPH winds. These storms, which primarily consisted of multicell clusters and short line segments, first started developing around sunset in northwest Kansas and southwest Nebraska near a surface trough axis before spreading east into the local area. On a positive note, this event brought widespread rainfall of at least 0.50-inch to much of the area, bringing limited short-term relief to the ongoing severe to extreme drought. On the large-scale aloft, this event unfolded within modest quasi-zonal flow between the southern periphery of a shortwave trough crossing the Northern Plains states and a mid-upper ridge centered over northern Texas. Near the time of the minor wind damage at Burr Oak, the mesoscale environment featured at least 1000 J/kg most-unstable CAPE and 30-40 knots of 0-6 kilometer wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several small branches down. The largest branch down was 8 inches in diameter, with the estimated 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather intensified the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of July. An unusually dry month of May and June combined with high temperatures soaring back above the century mark (100s) to push most of the region from a severe drought (D2) into an extreme drought (D3) by the end of the month of July.  Most areas saw precipitation fall well below normal for July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions began to worsen during the month of July, reaching extreme drought (D3) across South Central Kansas during the last part of the month.  Rainfall amounts were almost three inches below normal. The lack of significant rainfall combined with 15 consecutive days of maximum temperatures climbing above 100 degrees to scorch the crops and grasslands.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions were observed across the county. Rainfall was 3.67 inches in Atchison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system moved across the Central Plains states producing wind gusts of 50 to 65 mph from mid-morning through much of the afternoon.  Blowing dirt reduced visibility locally to less than 1/4 mile at times.  Minor damage occurred across the region.  A 14-inch diameter tree was split in two, at least one accident on Interstate 70 in Thomas County was attributed to the high winds and reduced visibility. In addition, a semi-trailer was also blown over in I-70.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The persistent upper level high pressure ridge over the central portions of the country which caused extremely dry conditions throughout the summer weakened and a more active pattern for rainfall occurred during the month of September. The rainfall from the remnants of Hurricane Isaac also helped with drought relief. Drought conditions began to improve over portions of extreme southeastern Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S Drought Monitor continued to report Severe Drought (D3) to Exceptional Drought (D4) throughout the month of September. The region saw more rainfall than previous months which helped the rainfall deficit. The COOP station near Fort Scott reported 6.33 of rainfall for the month of September. This is a continuation of the drought that began across the region in June. The loss and damage estimates will be included in August's storm data section for the entire drought event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During August 2012, rainfall was highly variable across this six-county area, with some places realizing below normal amounts of no more than 2 inches, while others managed to measure near normal to even above normal totals of at least 3-4. Some of the lowest monthly amounts per NWS cooperative observers including 0.56 near Long Island, 1.11 at Logan and 1.76 near Alton. On the wetter side of things were locations such as Webster Dam (4.59), near Plainville (3.93) and Beloit (3.59). However, over the longer-range period from June 1st-August 15th rainfall totals across most of the area only averaged between 45-70 percent of normal. Temperature-wise, after a notably hot June and especially July, readings in August eased up a bit with the mean monthly temperature across most of the area averaging 1-3 degrees below normal for a change. Despite these somewhat encouraging trends, the overall drought impact continued to retain or even worsen its grip during the month. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, most of the area started out August in category D3 extreme drought. By month's end, this extreme drought had overtaken even more of the area, with category D4 exceptional drought creeping into southern portions of Rooks and Osborne Counties. Only portions of eastern Jewell and northeast Mitchell Counties still held onto category D2 severe drought at the end of the month. This onset of severe to exceptional drought represented a notable degradation in conditions since early May when the entire area was free of all drought categories. Looking back, the drought situation that took initially took hold in May and June continued to worsen in July when most of the area measured no more than 1-2 inches of rain, versus the 30-year normal of around 4 inches. Furthermore, average maximum temperatures for the combined months of June and July ended up a significant 6-10 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. As of August 8th, the USDA had designated all six counties as primary counties in a federal drought disaster area. For the week ending August 12th, the Kansas Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that topsoil moisture across the north central district was 81 percent short to very short and subsoil moisture 91 percent short to very short. Across the state as a whole, range and pasture conditions were reported to be 90 percent poor to very poor, the worst since ratings began in 1995. However, grazing and haying of Conservation Reserve Program (CRP) acres was being taken advantage of to supplement depleted pastures. The Kansas corn and soybean crop was rated as 70 percent poor to very poor, marking the worst conditions since the current data series began in 1985.

Furthermore, average maximum temperatures for the combined months of June and July ended up a significant 6-10 degrees above normal, essentially baking moisture-starved maturing crops. As of August 8th, the USDA had designated all six counties as primary counties in a federal drought disaster area. For the week ending August 12th, the Kansas Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that topsoil moisture across the north central district was 81 percent short to very short and subsoil moisture 91 percent short to very short. Across the state as a whole, range and pasture conditions were reported to be 90 percent poor to very poor, the worst since ratings began in 1995. However, grazing and haying of Conservation Reserve Program (CRP) acres was being taken advantage of to supplement depleted pastures. The Kansas corn and soybean crop was rated as 70 percent poor to very poor, marking the worst conditions since the current data series began in 1985.

During August 2012, rainfall was highly variable across this six-county area, with some places realizing below normal amounts of no more than 2 inches, while others managed to measure near normal to even above normal totals of at least 3-4. Some of the lowest monthly amounts per NWS cooperative observers including 0.56 near Long Island, 1.11 at Logan and 1.76 near Alton. On the wetter side of things were locations such as Webster Dam (4.59), near Plainville (3.93) and Beloit (3.59). However, over the longer-range period from June 1st-August 15th rainfall totals across most of the area only averaged between 45-70 percent of normal. Temperature-wise, after a notably hot June and especially July, readings in August eased up a bit with the mean monthly temperature across most of the area averaging 1-3 degrees below normal for a change. Despite these somewhat encouraging trends, the overall drought impact continued to retain or even worsen its grip during the month. According to the U.S. Drought Monitor, most of the area started out August in category D3 extreme drought. By month's end, this extreme drought had overtaken even more of the area, with category D4 exceptional drought creeping into southern portions of Rooks and Osborne Counties. Only portions of eastern Jewell and northeast Mitchell Counties still held onto category D2 severe drought at the end of the month. A mid-level short wave trough approaching Kansas helped to lift an increasingly unstable early September airmass during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds blew down tree limbs up to 14 inches in diameter.  The high wind was accompanied by torrential rainfall and appears to have been the result of a microburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms formed across northeast and east central Kansas on September 7, 2012.  Much of the damage was confined to areas of Lyon, Coffey, and Anderson Counties as a bow-echo thunderstorm formed across Lyon County and moved eastward bringing 60+ mph winds and causing damage to trees, power lines, and outbuildings.  Aside from the strong wind these storms produced some 1 inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 8 inch tree limb broke near 12th Street and Chestnut in Emporia, Kansas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Colony 2 small out buildings were damaged and a few trees were down near the Intersection of 200 and Delaware.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak Convection formed a landspout in western Shawnee County near Dover, Kansas. No damage was reported from this weak, short-lived tornado, and it lasted for less than one minute.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a thin landspout 3 miles southeast of Dover, Kansas. It made intermittent ground contact for about a minute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions, which started in the spring, intensified during the month of August. Drought D2 and D3 conditions at the beginning of the month, increased to D3 and D4 conditions by the end of the month. Most locations by the end month, continued yearly rainfall deficits in the 10 to 15 inch range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Below normal precipitation continued through August, with D4 exceptional drought conditions across the county. Atchison reported 3.47 inches of rain for the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather intensified the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of July. An unusually dry month of May and June combined with high temperatures soaring back above the century mark (100s) to push most of the region from a severe drought (D2) into an extreme drought (D3) by the end of the month of July.  Most areas saw precipitation fall well below normal for July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions began to worsen during the month of July, reaching extreme drought (D3) across South Central Kansas during the first part of the month.  Rainfall amounts were three inches below normal in Wichita, Kansas. The lack of significant rainfall combined with 15 consecutive days of maximum temperatures above 100 degrees to scorch the crops and grasslands.  Maximum temperatures on two consecutive days reached 111 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions continued across all of the central plains through the month of September. Drought monitor for the central plains indicated that all of east central and north central Kansas reached D4 (Exceptional Drought) conditions, while far northeast Kansas reached D2 to D3 (Severe to Extreme Drought).  Dry conditions through the month caused the drought to be either steady-state or actually worsen in some locations.

The drought was persistent for the county through most of the month, as below average total precipitation fell across northeast Kansas throughout the month. The month started and ended with D3 (extreme) and D4 (exceptional) drought conditions.

Rainfall amounts continued below normal for most of the month, but a two and half inch rainfall late in the month led to some improvement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second consecutive month, rainfall totals during September were highly variable across these six counties, with limited areas benefiting from above normal amounts in excess of 2 inches, while most places again fell short of normal with totals closer to 1 inch or less. Given that the majority of the area remained firmly on the dry side, the overall drought impact tightened its grip during the month, plunging North Central Kansas even deeper into one of the worst droughts in several decades. As of the end of September, all six counties remained under a federally-designated drought disaster. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor, the coverage of category D4 exceptional drought expanded across the area during the month, encompassing all of Phillips, Rooks and Osborne counties by month's end, along with sizeable chunks of Smith and Mitchell counties. Most of Jewell and northeast Mitchell counties remained void of the exceptional drought category, but were still covered by a mix of category D3 extreme and category D2 severe drought. Most of the September rain fell during the first week in association with thunderstorms on the 4th and 6th, and as a whole, Phillips and Smith counties were the driest of the six counties during the month. Some of the lowest September rainfall totals per NWS cooperative observers included 0.36 inches near Long Island, 0.41 at Smith Center and 0.62 near Burr Oak. The limited areas that measured near-to-above normal amounts included Cawker City (3.60), Jewell (2.51) and Covert in rural Osborne County (2.44). ||Over the longer range five-month period from May through September, corresponding to the majority of the growing season, most of North Central Kansas only realized between 35-60 percent of normal rainfall. For the week ending September 23rd, the Kansas Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that topsoil moisture across the north central district was 59 percent short to very short and subsoil moisture 82 percent short to very short. Across the state as a whole, range and pasture conditions were reportedly 83 percent poor to very poor, the worst since ratings began in 1995. Both the soybean and sorghum crops were indicated as 67 percent poor to very poor.

For the second consecutive month, rainfall totals during September were highly variable across these six counties, with limited areas benefiting from above normal amounts in excess of 2 inches, while most places again fell short of normal with totals closer to 1 inch or less. Given that the majority of the area remained firmly on the dry side, the overall drought impact tightened its grip during the month, plunging North Central Kansas even deeper into one of the worst droughts in several decades. As of the end of September, all six counties remained under a federally-designated drought disaster. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor, the coverage of category D4 exceptional drought expanded across the area during the month, encompassing all of Phillips, Rooks and Osborne counties by month's end, along with sizeable chunks of Smith and Mitchell counties. Most of Jewell and northeast Mitchell counties remained void of the exceptional drought category, but were still covered by a mix of category D3 extreme and category D2 severe drought. Most of the September rain fell during the first week in association with thunderstorms on the 4th and 6th, and as a whole, Phillips and Smith counties were the driest of the six counties during the month. Some of the lowest September rainfall totals per NWS cooperative observers included 0.36 inches near Long Island, 0.41 at Smith Center and 0.62 near Burr Oak. The limited areas that measured near-to-above normal amounts included Cawker City (3.60), Jewell (2.51) and Covert in rural Osborne County (2.44). ||Over the longer range five-month period from May through September, corresponding to the majority of the growing season, most of North Central Kansas only realized between 35-60 percent of normal rainfall. For the week ending September 23rd, the Kansas Weather and Crops Report from the USDA indicated that topsoil moisture across the north central district was 59 percent short to very short and subsoil moisture 82 percent short to very short. Across the state as a whole, range and pasture conditions were reportedly 83 percent poor to very poor, the worst since ratings began in 1995. Both the soybean and sorghum crops were indicated as 67 percent poor to very poor.

A prolonged period of dry weather continued the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of September. An unusually dry period from May through August kept an exteme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of September.  Most areas saw some rainfall towards the end of the month, with a significant rainfall bringing almost one to two inches to most locations duirng the last week of September, which led to some gradual improvement over portions of the Flint Hills and Southeast Kansas, from an exceptional drought to an extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The exceptional drought (D4), continued across the Flint Hills and Southeast Kansas during the first part of the month of September. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of August and into September. Some welcome rainfall did return to the region by the end of the month, with some areas picking up over two inches of rainfall, which actually led to above normal rainfall amounts for the first time since April of 2012.  This also led to the drought level dropping to extreme (D3) levels by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather intensified the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of August. An unusually dry period from May through July combined with high temperatures soaring back above the century mark (100s) for the first part of the month, to push most of the region from an extreme drought (D3) into an exceptional drought (D4) by the end of the month of August. Most areas saw some rainfall towards the end of the month, which led to some gradual improvement over South Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of August. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of July and into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extended period of dry weather intensified the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of July. An unusually dry month of May and June combined with high temperatures soaring back above the century mark (100s) to push most of the region from a severe drought (D2) into an extreme drought (D3) by the end of the month of July.  Most areas saw precipitation fall well below normal for July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions began to rapidly worsen during the month of July, going from a severe drought (D2) across Southeast Kansas during the first part of the month to an extreme drought (D3) by the end of the month. Rainfall amounts were almost three inches below normal. The lack of significant rainfall combined with 15 consecutive days of maximum temperatures climbing above 100 degrees to scorch the crops and grasslands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coverage of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds ended up being more widespread than initially expected on this late Tuesday afternoon and early evening. Primarily between 5-8 PM CDT, a small quasi-linear complex of outflow-dominant multicell storms marched across most of this six-county area from west-to-east, resulting in several reports of damaging winds of 60-70 MPH and a few instances of small hail up to nickel size. A few of the most noteworthy reports included estimated 70 MPH winds near Woodston that knocked down several power poles and rolled 15-20 large hay bales across a road, and estimated 70 MPH winds in and near Downs that blew the second story off a house under construction and flattened several fences. Even as storms were exiting the far eastern edge of the area, estimated 60 MPH winds knocked a tree onto power lines at Formoso in Jewell County, sparking a house fire. Although most of the area measured well under 1 inch of rain from these storms, a localized pocket of heavy rain targeted parts of southern Jewell and northern Mitchell counties, highlighted by totals of 2.76 inches at Cawker City and 1.95 at Jewell, where nickel size hail also fell. ||The initial development of isolated, high-based multicell storms occurred over extreme northwest Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours along a subtle surface trough axis extending southwestward from north central Nebraska, with this activity gradually morphing into a quasi-linear complex as it charged east into North Central Kansas. In the mid and upper levels, flow was quasi-zonal and modest in strength, but contained weak embedded shortwave troughs well to the south of a much stronger wave sliding through eastern Montana. Although 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear was only around 30 knots, surface-based CAPE up to around 1000 J/kg in the presence of a hot and deeply-mixed airmass with surface temperatures in the upper-90s and dewpoints only in the 50s resulted in a favorable environment for damaging outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated near 60 mph resulted in tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Primarily between 1 AM and 5 AM CDT on this early Thursday morning, a small cluster of elevated multicell storms tracked across parts of the six-county area, mainly affecting Phillips, Smith, and Jewell Counties. While radar data suggested that most of this activity was fairly weak, a briefly strong storm covered the ground with penny to nickel size hail in Lebanon around 330 AM CDT. Although this storm continued eastward into Jewell County, no further hail reports were received. These nocturnal storms flared up in the wake of the first significant cold front of the fall season, within a zone of pronounced low-mid level frontogenesis trailing southward from a strong shortwave trough passing through the eastern Dakotas. Around the time of the Lebanon hail, mesoscale parameters indicated that storms were taking full advantage of only around 500 J/kg most-unstable CAPE, but increasing 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear of 30-40 knots. Behind this stout cold front, surface temperatures on the 4th in North Central Kansas only reached around 60��, compared to mid-upper 80s the previous day.

Continuing a trend from recent months, rainfall during October exhibited considerable variability within these six counties. However, given that most of the area finished below normal for the sixth consecutive month, the overall drought situation stayed mostly unchanged as the summer and fall of 2012 continued marking its place in history as one of the worst droughts in several decades. Throughout the month, all six counties continued under a federally-designated drought disaster. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor, category D4 exceptional drought remained firmly in place across Phillips and Rooks counties, along with the majority of Smith and Osborne counties. Meanwhile, a slight improvement was noted in Jewell and Mitchell counties, as this area ended the month with a mix of category D3 extreme and category D2 severe drought. Taking a closer look at monthly precipitation totals, the majority of Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties measured between 0.60-1.25 inches, compared to October normals generally between 1.70-2.00. Put another way, most of these areas only realized between 35-70 percent of normal October rainfall. Some of the lowest monthly totals per NWS cooperative observers included 0.51 at Webster Dam, 0.60 near Long Island and 0.76 at Kirwin Dam. Farther east, most of Jewell and Mitchell counties tallied higher rainfall totals between 1.75-2.25, with a few of the largest amounts including 3.16 near Burr Oak and 2.27 at Beloit. The majority of October rain was associated with thunderstorms on the 13th and 14th. ||Over the longer range period from May through mid-October, most of North Central Kansas only realized between 35-60 percent of normal rainfall. As just one example, Smith Center measured only 6.73 during this 5 �� month time span...or 37% of the normal amount of 17.88. For the week ending October 14th, the Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report from the USDA indicated that topsoil moisture across the north central district was 49 percent short to very short and subsoil moisture 78 percent short to very short. Across the state as a whole, range and pasture conditions were reportedly 80 percent poor to very poor, the worst since ratings began in 1985. Both hay and stock water supplies rated around 70 percent short to very short, a figure that has held fairly steady through the past few months. Poor to very poor crop conditions across the state were assigned to 67 percent of sorghum and 61 percent of soybeans. Ninety percent of the statewide corn harvest was already complete, about three weeks ahead of average.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to sleet and snow from northwest to southeast across much of northeast Kansas.  The rain changed to snow after 9pm across north central Kansas and after midnight across the rest of northeast Kansas.  The snow was heavy at times and many areas received between 2 and 6 inches of snow.  What was most notable about this storm was the strong northwest winds that gusted to over 50 mph at times.  This created near blizzard conditions for a 3-6 hour period during the pre-dawn hours of December 20th.  The decision to verify winter storm conditions was based off of the combined impacts of the falling snow and strong winds that created near blizzard conditions during the early morning rush hour of December 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 2 to 4 inches of snow across Riley County with the heaviest amounts in the northern half of the county.  Winds gusting to 50 mph created near blizzard conditions through sunrise.

However, a small portion of western Leavenworth County had D3 conditions. Rainfall deficits for the year were generally in the 10 to 16 inch range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather intensified the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of August. An unusually dry period from May through July combined with high temperatures soaring back above the century mark (100s) for the first part of the month, to push most of the region from an extreme drought (D3) into an exceptional drought (D4) by the end of the month of August. Most areas saw some rainfall towards the end of the month, which led to some gradual improvement over South Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions began to worsen during the month of August, reaching exceptional drought (D4) across Central Kansas during the middle part of the month.  Rainfall amounts were almost one inch below normal. The lack of significant rainfall combined with days of maximum temperatures climbing above 100 degrees early in the month to scorch the crops and grasslands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather continued the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of September. An unusually dry period from May through August kept an exteme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of September.  Most areas saw some rainfall towards the end of the month, with a significant rainfall bringing almost one to two inches to most locations duirng the last week of September, which led to some gradual improvement over portions of the Flint Hills and Southeast Kansas, from an exceptional drought to an extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe drought (D3), continued across Southeast Kansas during the month of September. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of August and into September. Some welcome rainfall did return to the region by the end of the month, with some areas picking up over two inches of rainfall, which actually led to above normal rainfall amounts for the first time since April of 2012.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of Central Kansas during the afternoon of September 4th, 2012.  The hot and humid airmass in place, led to prime conditions for damaging downburst winds from the high based thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The severe wind gusts occurred over 8 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions continued across all of the central plains through the month of September. Drought monitor for the central plains indicated that all of east central and north central Kansas reached D4 (Exceptional Drought) conditions, while far northeast Kansas reached D2 to D3 (Severe to Extreme Drought).  Dry conditions through the month caused the drought to be either steady-state or actually worsen in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across Clay County continued from August's dry weather, as the arid conditions continued into September. The drought was persistent for the county through most of the month, as below average total precipitation fell across northeast Kansas throughout the month. The month started and ended with D3 (extreme) drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to sleet and snow from northwest to southeast across much of northeast Kansas.  The rain changed to snow after 9pm across north central Kansas and after midnight across the rest of northeast Kansas.  The snow was heavy at times and many areas received between 2 and 6 inches of snow.  What was most notable about this storm was the strong northwest winds that gusted to over 50 mph at times.  This created near blizzard conditions for a 3-6 hour period during the pre-dawn hours of December 20th.  The decision to verify winter storm conditions was based off of the combined impacts of the falling snow and strong winds that created near blizzard conditions during the early morning rush hour of December 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 3 to 6 inches of snow across Nemaha County with the heaviest amounts in the northern half of the county.  Sleet was also reported at the onset of the snow around midnight.  Winds gusting to 50 mph created near blizzard conditions through sunrise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coverage of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds ended up being more widespread than initially expected on this late Tuesday afternoon and early evening. Primarily between 5-8 PM CDT, a small quasi-linear complex of outflow-dominant multicell storms marched across most of this six-county area from west-to-east, resulting in several reports of damaging winds of 60-70 MPH and a few instances of small hail up to nickel size. A few of the most noteworthy reports included estimated 70 MPH winds near Woodston that knocked down several power poles and rolled 15-20 large hay bales across a road, and estimated 70 MPH winds in and near Downs that blew the second story off a house under construction and flattened several fences. Even as storms were exiting the far eastern edge of the area, estimated 60 MPH winds knocked a tree onto power lines at Formoso in Jewell County, sparking a house fire. Although most of the area measured well under 1 inch of rain from these storms, a localized pocket of heavy rain targeted parts of southern Jewell and northern Mitchell counties, highlighted by totals of 2.76 inches at Cawker City and 1.95 at Jewell, where nickel size hail also fell. ||The initial development of isolated, high-based multicell storms occurred over extreme northwest Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours along a subtle surface trough axis extending southwestward from north central Nebraska, with this activity gradually morphing into a quasi-linear complex as it charged east into North Central Kansas. In the mid and upper levels, flow was quasi-zonal and modest in strength, but contained weak embedded shortwave troughs well to the south of a much stronger wave sliding through eastern Montana. Although 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear was only around 30 knots, surface-based CAPE up to around 1000 J/kg in the presence of a hot and deeply-mixed airmass with surface temperatures in the upper-90s and dewpoints only in the 50s resulted in a favorable environment for damaging outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated near 70 mph damaged around 40 power poles, resulting in the loss of power in town, and blew 15 to 20 hay bales across a road.

Mainly between 10 PM and 2 AM CDT on this Saturday evening and early Sunday morning, scattered to numerous multicell storms rumbled through much of the six-county area before gradually weakening and departing to the southeast. The most widespread storm coverage, including a few that became marginally severe, targeted Jewell and Mitchell Counties where quarter size hail fell in both Burr Oak and Beloit. Rainfall totals in excess of one inch were also measured in a few spots, including 1.92 at Burr Oak and 1.26 at Lebanon. Earlier in the afternoon between 5-6 PM CDT, the local area narrowly missed out on a separate corridor of strong to severe storms that streamed from central Kansas into southeast Nebraska within a zone of enhanced deep-layer wind shear just east of a surface cold front. Although the western edge of this activity actually clipped far southeastern Mitchell County, there were no storm reports from that area. Both the afternoon and evening events were driven by a rather deep mid-upper level trough that lumbered directly over the Central Plains from west to east. As the core of this upper trough axis passed overhead after nightfall, lingering elevated CAPE on the order of 500-1000 J/kg along with pronounced mid level convergence sparked these locally severe storms.
An intense surface low helped generate widespread strong wind with high wind reports scattered about.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 MPH was measured at a location 3 miles WNW of Jetmore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense surface low helped generate widespread strong wind with high wind reports scattered about.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust ot 58 MPH was measured at a location near Wakeeney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly between 6 PM and 830 PM CDT on this Thursday evening, two severe thunderstorms roamed parts of the area, primarily impacting Rooks and Smith counties. Starting with the Smith County storm, this was an organized, southeast-tracking supercell that crossed into Kansas out of Franklin County Nebraska around 630 PM CDT and eventually weakened near the southern end of the Smith-Jewell county line around 8 PM CDT. As it tracked through Smith County, this storm produced a measured 61 MPH gust at the Smith Center airport and nickel size hail at Bellaire, although radar signatures suggest larger hail likely fell north of Smith Center near the Nebraska border. This storm also showed signs of low-level rotation for a short time, although no tornadoes were reported.  The Rooks County storm was more of a multicell cluster as it entered the county from the west around 6 PM CDT and gradually weakened over southern Osborne County between 730-830 PM CDT. Along the way, 60-65 MPH winds were reported in western Rooks County, including a 64 MPH measured gust north of Damar. The dissipating remnants of both storms completely exited Jewell and Mitchell counties by 930 PM CDT. ||On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by the invasion of a subtle shortwave trough embedded in seasonably strong quasi-zonal flow, well south of a primary mid level trough axis extending from Ontario to Montana. At the surface, this storm flared up along a rather well defined pre-frontal trough/dryline extending southward from north central Nebraska. This boundary marked a sharp moisture gradient, separating dewpoints in the 20s-30s F to the west from dewpoints in the upper 50s-lower 60s to the east. Early evening mesoscale severe weather parameters in North Central Kansas featured around 1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 40 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts were estimated at 60 to 65 mph, blowing down small tree limbs.

Although most of the area measured well under 1 inch of rain from these storms, a localized pocket of heavy rain targeted parts of southern Jewell and northern Mitchell counties, highlighted by totals of 2.76 inches at Cawker City and 1.95 at Jewell, where nickel size hail also fell. ||The initial development of isolated, high-based multicell storms occurred over extreme northwest Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours along a subtle surface trough axis extending southwestward from north central Nebraska, with this activity gradually morphing into a quasi-linear complex as it charged east into North Central Kansas. In the mid and upper levels, flow was quasi-zonal and modest in strength, but contained weak embedded shortwave troughs well to the south of a much stronger wave sliding through eastern Montana. Although 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear was only around 30 knots, surface-based CAPE up to around 1000 J/kg in the presence of a hot and deeply-mixed airmass with surface temperatures in the upper-90s and dewpoints only in the 50s resulted in a favorable environment for damaging outflow winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated near 60 mph knocked over a 12 inch diameter tree onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coverage of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds ended up being more widespread than initially expected on this late Tuesday afternoon and early evening. Primarily between 5-8 PM CDT, a small quasi-linear complex of outflow-dominant multicell storms marched across most of this six-county area from west-to-east, resulting in several reports of damaging winds of 60-70 MPH and a few instances of small hail up to nickel size. A few of the most noteworthy reports included estimated 70 MPH winds near Woodston that knocked down several power poles and rolled 15-20 large hay bales across a road, and estimated 70 MPH winds in and near Downs that blew the second story off a house under construction and flattened several fences. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly between 10 PM and 2 AM CDT on this Saturday evening and early Sunday morning, scattered to numerous multicell storms rumbled through much of the six-county area before gradually weakening and departing to the southeast. The most widespread storm coverage, including a few that became marginally severe, targeted Jewell and Mitchell Counties where quarter size hail fell in both Burr Oak and Beloit. Rainfall totals in excess of one inch were also measured in a few spots, including 1.92 at Burr Oak and 1.26 at Lebanon. Earlier in the afternoon between 5-6 PM CDT, the local area narrowly missed out on a separate corridor of strong to severe storms that streamed from central Kansas into southeast Nebraska within a zone of enhanced deep-layer wind shear just east of a surface cold front. Although the western edge of this activity actually clipped far southeastern Mitchell County, there were no storm reports from that area. Both the afternoon and evening events were driven by a rather deep mid-upper level trough that lumbered directly over the Central Plains from west to east. As the core of this upper trough axis passed overhead after nightfall, lingering elevated CAPE on the order of 500-1000 J/kg along with pronounced mid level convergence sparked these locally severe storms.

Continuing a trend from recent months, rainfall during October exhibited considerable variability within these six counties. However, given that most of the area finished below normal for the sixth consecutive month, the overall drought situation stayed mostly unchanged as the summer and fall of 2012 continued marking its place in history as one of the worst droughts in several decades. Throughout the month, all six counties continued under a federally-designated drought disaster. According to weekly issuances of the U.S. Drought Monitor, category D4 exceptional drought remained firmly in place across Phillips and Rooks counties, along with the majority of Smith and Osborne counties. Meanwhile, a slight improvement was noted in Jewell and Mitchell counties, as this area ended the month with a mix of category D3 extreme and category D2 severe drought. Taking a closer look at monthly precipitation totals, the majority of Phillips, Rooks, Smith and Osborne counties measured between 0.60-1.25 inches, compared to October normals generally between 1.70-2.00. Put another way, most of these areas only realized between 35-70 percent of normal October rainfall. Some of the lowest monthly totals per NWS cooperative observers included 0.51 at Webster Dam, 0.60 near Long Island and 0.76 at Kirwin Dam. Farther east, most of Jewell and Mitchell counties tallied higher rainfall totals between 1.75-2.25, with a few of the largest amounts including 3.16 near Burr Oak and 2.27 at Beloit. The majority of October rain was associated with thunderstorms on the 13th and 14th. ||Over the longer range period from May through mid-October, most of North Central Kansas only realized between 35-60 percent of normal rainfall. As just one example, Smith Center measured only 6.73 during this 5 �� month time span...or 37% of the normal amount of 17.88. For the week ending October 14th, the Kansas Crop Progress and Condition Report from the USDA indicated that topsoil moisture across the north central district was 49 percent short to very short and subsoil moisture 78 percent short to very short. Across the state as a whole, range and pasture conditions were reportedly 80 percent poor to very poor, the worst since ratings began in 1985. Both hay and stock water supplies rated around 70 percent short to very short, a figure that has held fairly steady through the past few months. Poor to very poor crop conditions across the state were assigned to 67 percent of sorghum and 61 percent of soybeans. Ninety percent of the statewide corn harvest was already complete, about three weeks ahead of average.

A prolonged period of dry weather continued the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of September. An unusually dry period from May through August kept an exteme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of September.  Most areas saw some rainfall towards the end of the month, with a significant rainfall bringing almost one to two inches to most locations duirng the last week of September, which led to some gradual improvement over portions of the Flint Hills and Southeast Kansas, from an exceptional drought to an extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions continued during the month of September across Central Kansas. Some welcome rainfall did occur late in the month with some areas picking up almost an inch and a quarter of rain (1.25 in.). But rainfall amounts were still almost a half an inch below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather intensified the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of August. An unusually dry period from May through July combined with high temperatures soaring back above the century mark (100s) for the first part of the month, to push most of the region from an extreme drought (D3) into an exceptional drought (D4) by the end of the month of August. Most areas saw some rainfall towards the end of the month, which led to some gradual improvement over South Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme drought (D3), continued across South Central Kansas during the month of August. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of July and into August.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to sleet and snow from northwest to southeast across much of northeast Kansas.  The rain changed to snow after 9pm across north central Kansas and after midnight across the rest of northeast Kansas.  The snow was heavy at times and many areas received between 2 and 6 inches of snow.  What was most notable about this storm was the strong northwest winds that gusted to over 50 mph at times.  This created near blizzard conditions for a 3-6 hour period during the pre-dawn hours of December 20th.  The decision to verify winter storm conditions was based off of the combined impacts of the falling snow and strong winds that created near blizzard conditions during the early morning rush hour of December 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 3 to 5 inches of snow across Marshall County.  Winds gusting to 50 mph created near blizzard conditions through sunrise.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed and moved southeast across parts of northwest Kansas during the late afternoon and evening.  Two separate lightning strikes in extreme southern Sherman County resulted in fires which eventually merged into a large fire consuming 15,000 acres.  One abandoned farm house was destroyed, otherwise no structures or machinery were affected.  The fire lasted approximately 8 hours before it was deemed under control.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large grass fire resulted from two lightning strikes which occurred during the late afternoon.  Erratic, gusty winds from thunderstorm outflows resulted in the fire escalating to eventually cover 15,000 acres in southern Sherman county along the Wallace county border.  One abandoned farm house was destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across Osage County continued from August's dry weather, as the arid conditions continued into September. Although Emporia received above normal rainfall amounts for the month the drought was persistent for the county. The month started and ended wit D3 (extreme) to D4 (exceptional) drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain changed to sleet and snow from northwest to southeast across much of northeast Kansas.  The rain changed to snow after 9pm across north central Kansas and after midnight across the rest of northeast Kansas.  The snow was heavy at times and many areas received between 2 and 6 inches of snow.  What was most notable about this storm was the strong northwest winds that gusted to over 50 mph at times.  This created near blizzard conditions for a 3-6 hour period during the pre-dawn hours of December 20th.  The decision to verify winter storm conditions was based off of the combined impacts of the falling snow and strong winds that created near blizzard conditions during the early morning rush hour of December 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotters reported 2 to 6 inches of snow across Pottawatomie County with the heaviest amounts in the northern half of the county.  Sleet was also reported at the onset of the snow around midnight.  Winds gusting to 50 mph created near blizzard conditions through sunrise.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management and spotters reported a wide range of snow estimates and measurements.  Anywhere from 2 to as much as 4 inches of snow was reported across the county.  However, strong winds gusting to as high as 50 mph created near blizzard conditions between midnight and 6 am.  The impacts of the snow and winds are the basis for the decision to verify the winter storm warning for Jefferson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions continued from August with all of northwest Kansas rated as being in Exceptional (D4) Drought.  Year-to-date precipitation through the end of September averaged 40% to 80% of normal.

An extended period of dry weather intensified the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of July. An unusually dry month of May and June combined with high temperatures soaring back above the century mark (100s) to push most of the region from a severe drought (D2) into an extreme drought (D3) by the end of the month of July.  Most areas saw precipitation fall well below normal for July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions began to worsen during the month of July, reaching extreme drought (D3) across Central Kansas during the first part of the month.  Rainfall amounts were almost three inches below normal. The lack of significant rainfall combined with 15 consecutive days of maximum temperatures climbing above 100 degrees to scorch the crops and grasslands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather continued the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of September. An unusually dry period from May through August kept an exteme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of September.  Most areas saw some rainfall towards the end of the month, with a significant rainfall bringing almost one to two inches to most locations duirng the last week of September, which led to some gradual improvement over portions of the Flint Hills and Southeast Kansas, from an exceptional drought to an extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The exceptional drought (D4), continued across Southeast Kansas during the first part of the month of September. This was a continuation of a drought that started in late May and continued through the month of August and into September. Some welcome rainfall did return to the region by the end of the month, with some areas picking up over two inches of rainfall, which actually led to above normal rainfall amounts for the first time since April of 2012.  This also led to the drought level dropping to extreme (D3) levels by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central and Southeast Kansas during the afternoon hours of September 7th, 2012.  The warm and moist airmass was located north of cold front that was located over northeast Oklahoma.  The severe storms initially were damaging wind producers along a bowing line segment, but a few elevated supercells developed over the city of Wichita, Kansas, produced hail up to 2 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees and large branches were blown down, along with some power lines knocked down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coverage of severe thunderstorms with damaging winds ended up being more widespread than initially expected on this late Tuesday afternoon and early evening. Primarily between 5-8 PM CDT, a small quasi-linear complex of outflow-dominant multicell storms marched across most of this six-county area from west-to-east, resulting in several reports of damaging winds of 60-70 MPH and a few instances of small hail up to nickel size. A few of the most noteworthy reports included estimated 70 MPH winds near Woodston that knocked down several power poles and rolled 15-20 large hay bales across a road, and estimated 70 MPH winds in and near Downs that blew the second story off a house under construction and flattened several fences. Even as storms were exiting the far eastern edge of the area, estimated 60 MPH winds knocked a tree onto power lines at Formoso in Jewell County, sparking a house fire. Although most of the area measured well under 1 inch of rain from these storms, a localized pocket of heavy rain targeted parts of southern Jewell and northern Mitchell counties, highlighted by totals of 2.76 inches at Cawker City and 1.95 at Jewell, where nickel size hail also fell. ||The initial development of isolated, high-based multicell storms occurred over extreme northwest Kansas during the mid-afternoon hours along a subtle surface trough axis extending southwestward from north central Nebraska, with this activity gradually morphing into a quasi-linear complex as it charged east into North Central Kansas. In the mid and upper levels, flow was quasi-zonal and modest in strength, but contained weak embedded shortwave troughs well to the south of a much stronger wave sliding through eastern Montana. Although 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear was only around 30 knots, surface-based CAPE up to around 1000 J/kg in the presence of a hot and deeply-mixed airmass with surface temperatures in the upper-90s and dewpoints only in the 50s resulted in a favorable environment for damaging outflow winds.

Most areas saw some rainfall towards the end of the month, which led to some gradual improvement over South Central Kansas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions began to worsen during the month of August, reaching exceptional drought (D4) across Central Kansas during the middle part of the month.  Rainfall amounts were almost one inch below normal. The lack of significant rainfall combined with days of maximum temperatures climbing above 100 degrees early in the month to scorch the crops and grasslands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of dry weather continued the drought conditions across most of Kansas during the month of September. An unusually dry period from May through August kept an exteme drought (D3) going for most of Southern Kansas. Portions of Central Kansas continued to stay very dry, with exceptional drought (D4) conditions for most of the month of September.  Most areas saw some rainfall towards the end of the month, with a significant rainfall bringing almost one to two inches to most locations duirng the last week of September, which led to some gradual improvement over portions of the Flint Hills and Southeast Kansas, from an exceptional drought to an extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Exceptional Drought (D4) conditions continued during the month of September  across Central Kansas. Some welcome rainfall did occur late in the month with some areas picking up almost an inch and a quarter of rain (1.25 in.). But rainfall amounts were still almost a half an inch below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mainly between 6 PM and 830 PM CDT on this Thursday evening, two severe thunderstorms roamed parts of the area, primarily impacting Rooks and Smith counties. Starting with the Smith County storm, this was an organized, southeast-tracking supercell that crossed into Kansas out of Franklin County Nebraska around 630 PM CDT and eventually weakened near the southern end of the Smith-Jewell county line around 8 PM CDT. As it tracked through Smith County, this storm produced a measured 61 MPH gust at the Smith Center airport and nickel size hail at Bellaire, although radar signatures suggest larger hail likely fell north of Smith Center near the Nebraska border. This storm also showed signs of low-level rotation for a short time, although no tornadoes were reported.  The Rooks County storm was more of a multicell cluster as it entered the county from the west around 6 PM CDT and gradually weakened over southern Osborne County between 730-830 PM CDT. Along the way, 60-65 MPH winds were reported in western Rooks County, including a 64 MPH measured gust north of Damar. The dissipating remnants of both storms completely exited Jewell and Mitchell counties by 930 PM CDT. ||On the synoptic scale aloft, this event was driven by the invasion of a subtle shortwave trough embedded in seasonably strong quasi-zonal flow, well south of a primary mid level trough axis extending from Ontario to Montana. At the surface, this storm flared up along a rather well defined pre-frontal trough/dryline extending southward from north central Nebraska. This boundary marked a sharp moisture gradient, separating dewpoints in the 20s-30s F to the west from dewpoints in the upper 50s-lower 60s to the east. Early evening mesoscale severe weather parameters in North Central Kansas featured around 1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE and 40 knots of 0-6 kilometer vertical wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts estimated at 60 mph were accompanied by pea size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated severe thunderstorms developed across portions of South Central and Southeast Kansas during the afternoon hours of September 7th, 2012.  The warm and moist airmass was located north of cold front that was located over northeast Oklahoma.  The severe storms initially were damaging wind producers along a bowing line segment, but a few elevated supercells developed over the city of Wichita, Kansas, produced hail up to 2 inches in diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage reported across town.

Thunderstorms developed over Rawlins and Decatur counties during the afternoon and moved southeast.  Several reports of severe wind and hail were received, in addition to reports of heavy rainfall.  No flooding was reported, but precipitation totals for the 24-hour period ranged from 2.50 to 5.50.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated peak winds to be near 60 mph. Precipitation totaled 2.30 inches in about 50 minutes ending at 610 pm CDT..

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed along a strong cold front as it moved east across eastern Kansas. Portions of the squall line developed bow segments which produced strong winds with reports up to 80mph. Numerous reports of wind damage were received as the storms moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 70 mph wind gust was reported at Highway 13 and Highway 32.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot and dry conditions continued across all of the central plains through the month of August. Drought monitor for the central plains indicated that all of east central Kansas reached D4 (Exceptional Drought) conditions, while north central and far northeast Kansas reached D2 to D3 (Severe to Extreme Drought).  Conditions did improve toward the end of the month for a large portion of northeast Kansas as a long period of moderate to heavy rain brought 3 to 5 inches of rain.  This helped make up some of the rainfall deficit, but did not fully allow the area to catch up to average rainfall totals.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across Brown County continued from July's hot and dry weather, as the arid conditions continued into August. The drought worsened for the county through the first part of the month, but improved a little toward the end after a round of moderate to heavy rain brought much needed moisture to the area. As of this posting damage totals had not been received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong ridge of high pressure settled over the central portions of the U.S. beginning in June and became the dominant weather pattern for much of the Summer of 2012. This weather pattern finally broke down after the first week of August and temperatures became more seasonable. The overall mean temperatures for Joplin was 0.5 degrees below normal for the month of August.  The hottest temperatures occurred on August 2nd and 4th at 107 degrees. There were 6 days where the the maximum temperature reached 100 degrees or higher and this occurred during the first week of the month.  There were 20 days where the maximum temperatures reached 90 degrees or above. Heat advisories and warnings were issued for portions of the area for the early portion of August. Even though air temperatures were extreme, the humidity levels were relatively dry causing the heat index to be closer to the actual air temperatures.

The overall mean temperatures for Joplin was 0.5 degrees below normal for the month of August.  The hottest temperatures occurred on August 2nd and 4th at 107 degrees. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line developed along a strong cold front as it moved east across eastern Kansas. Portions of the squall line developed bow segments which produced strong winds with reports up to 80mph. Numerous reports of wind damage were received as the storms moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power lines were blown down in Girard. Winds were estimated between 60 to 70 mph and pea sized hail was also reported.
